SRET has the highest expense ratio, the lowest trading volumes, the highest turnover, the lowest total return and the highest risk measured by drawdown and volatility. Showing the highest yield doesn’t offset all these shortcomings. REET is leading the pack based on both return and risk-adjusted performance (Sharpe ratio in the table above).

Takeaway

Global X SuperDividend REIT ETF has a 8% yield from 30 global REITs. SRET has 67% of asset value in U.S. issuers and 34% in mortgage REITs. SRET has lost 54% in value and 49% in distribution since 2015. It has lagged benchmarks and the largest global REIT ETFs. Investors seeking exposure in global real estate can find a more compelling alternative in REET, with higher historical return and liquidity, and lower fees and volatility.

I renew my “Sell” rating for SRET, with a cautionary note: I disapprove of short-selling due to high yield and volatility.

data.title: BXSY: This Unique Fund Could Have A Lot To Offer
data.description:
The Bexil Investment Trust (OTCPK:BXSY) is a closed-end fund that investors who are in need of income might choose to add to their portfolios in an attempt to achieve their goals. The fund manages to do a fairly good job at providing its investors with a high level of current income, as its 7.32% current yield is significantly higher than the yields of any of the major domestic fixed-income or debt indices. We can see that in this chart:

Index/ETF

TTM Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.84%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.55%

Bloomberg US Floating Rate Note < 5 Yrs. Index (FLOT)

5.24%

Bloomberg U.S. Convertible Cash Pay Bond > $250MM Index (ICVT)

2.04%

This fund also delivers a substantially higher yield than any of the major domestic equity indices. We can see that here:

Index/ETF

TTM Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.13%

NASDAQ-100 Index (QQQ)

0.50%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

1.09%

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA)

1.50%

As we can see, none of the major domestic equity indices currently has yields in excess of 2%. Even the Dow Jones Industrial Average, which consists of mature blue-chip stocks that are generally long past their high-growth stage, is only yielding a meager 1.50% at its current level. This means that even a $1 million investment in one of the major equity indices would generate $15,000 per year at most. The majority of these indices would pay even less, as the same $1 million invested in the NASDAQ-100 Index would only generate $5,000 in annual income. This is hardly a very good income for anyone with a multimillion-dollar portfolio, and it clearly shows us how stretched valuations have become over the past several years. As such, most investors who are looking for income have been forced into the fixed-income market, but fixed-income securities have problems of their own right now, the most important of which is that they provide no protection against the near-certainty of a U.S. dollar devaluation in the medium term.

The Bexil Investment Trust could offer a way to solve both of these problems, as it offers a respectable yield while still providing investors with some protection against inflation or a currency devaluation. This is because of the strategy that the fund employs, which is described on its fact sheet thusly:

Under normal circumstances, at least 50% of its total assets are invested in income-generating equity securities.

In other words, the Bexil Investment Trust invests at least half of its assets in dividend-paying stocks. The rest of the fund could theoretically be invested in fixed-income securities, which would provide a higher level of current income for the fund at the expense of greatly reducing the ability of its portfolio to preserve the purchasing power of its investors’ capital. However, that does not appear to be the case right now, as the fund’s most recent holdings report states that it is entirely invested in equity securities. This report provides the following asset allocation as of March 31, 2025:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Common Stocks

114.15%

The holdings report does not list a money market fund, which is strange, as most closed-end funds maintain a small amount of cash for making opportunistic investments, paying distributions, or for other purposes where a ready supply of cash might be reasonable. This fund, however, appears to be fully invested in common stocks and maintains a line of credit that it can tap for cash as needed. This is not necessarily a bad thing, since having a large cash position does reduce the overall return of the portfolio due to cash usually delivering a lower return than common stocks, but it is more expensive to tap a line of credit for funding than it is to have cash on hand, so it is a trade-off. Fortunately, though, closed-end funds do not need to keep cash on hand to redeem shares when an investor wants to exit a position in the fund, so this, at least, is not a problem that we really need to worry about.

The fact that the Bexil Investment Trust primarily invests in dividend-paying equities is a very appealing characteristic. As regular readers are likely well aware, I am quite concerned with the very strong likelihood that the United States will be forced to devalue the U.S. dollar in the near future. As Rick Rule, founder of Rule Investment Media, points out, the United States government currently has off-balance sheet liabilities exceeding $100 trillion that primarily come from promises made to retirees to provide them with an income and healthcare in their golden years. No money was ever set aside to fund these promises, and they have started to become due. The only politically palatable way for the U.S. government to actually make good on these promises is to essentially print the money to pay for them, which will devalue the U.S. dollar and cause inflation.

According to AllianceBernstein, dividend-paying common equities have historically been a pretty good way to protect the purchasing power of assets in such an environment. Dividend-paying equities have a positive beta with inflation:

We can see that things such as the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and U.S. Treasury bonds had a negative beta with inflation going back to 1971. This is probably due to the fact that interest rates usually rise during such an environment, which causes high-growth stocks to decline in price. The same is true with U.S. Treasuries, as rising interest rates cause bond prices to fall significantly. A positive beta, in this case, means that the asset price goes up when inflation goes up, and we can see that this has historically only been the case with high-dividend paying stocks (defined as the second-highest quintile of the S&P 500 Index in terms of yield). While it is true that gold has historically done a better job at protecting investors against inflation, as have commodities, neither gold nor commodities provides any yield, so it is quite difficult to actually use those assets to generate an income. This leaves dividend-paying stocks as the asset class that income-seeking investors should use to meet their income needs.

As we will see in this article, the Bexil Investment Trust’s portfolio includes a number of dividend-paying stocks, so this may make it worth considering for investors who are looking to protect themselves against further bursts of inflation going forward while receiving an income.

Peer Distribution Comparison

As we just saw, the Bexil Investment Trust has a very attractive yield when compared to the domestic equity indices. The fund also compares very well to the domestic fixed-income indices in terms of yield. However, closed-end funds in general tend to boast substantially higher yields than most indices, so we should take a moment to see whether or not their yield remains attractive when we compare it to some of the fund’s peers. We can see just such a comparison in this chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Bexil Investment Trust

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

7.32%

Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund (ERH)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

8.10%

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

6.68%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

8.15%

Franklin Universal Trust (FT)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

6.54%

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (HTD)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

7.90%

Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund (NIE)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

8.32%

We can see here that the yield of the Bexil Investment Trust is generally comparable to the yields of its peers, although it is not the highest-yielding fund. The median yield of this peer group is 7.90% yield of the John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund, so we can see that the Bexil Investment Trust has a yield that is below the peer median level. Most of these funds have yields that are within a fairly tight range, though, and the fact remains that the yield of the Bexil Investment Trust is only 58 basis points below the peer median. On a $1 million investment, that only works out to $5,800 of extra income annually that an investor would get by purchasing shares of the John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund compared to the Bexil Investment Trust. This is only $483 per month, and that might not be enough for an investor to really be concerned about if the fund has enough other advantages going for it. The John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund currently has 44.47% of its assets invested in utilities compared to the 0% allocation to utilities in the Bexil Investment Trust, so that might sway investors in one direction or another, depending on what else is in their portfolios.

The price performance of the Bexil Investment Trust has also been very solid compared to the other funds in the peer group over the past five years. We can see that here:

Unfortunately, the chart cuts off some of the figures due to size constraints, so here are the same figures in table format:

Fund Name

Trailing 5-Year Price Performance

Bexil Investment Trust

27.68%

Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund

-10.15%

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund

40.47%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

22.53%

Franklin Universal Trust

13.25%

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

24.92%

Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund

1.65%

We can see that the Bexil Investment Trust was the second-best performing fund out of this peer group over the past five years. Or, at least, it was the second-best-performing fund on a price basis. Closed-end funds tend to deliver the bulk of their total returns through distributions made to their owners as opposed to depending on their own share prices rising over time. As such, it is a good idea for us to look at the total returns of these funds as opposed to their price performance alone. That comparison is visible in this chart:

Once again, here is the same data in table format for better visualization:

Fund Name

Trailing 5-Year Total Return

Bexil Investment Trust

110.19%

Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund

30.65%

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund

106.83%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

81.18%

Franklin Universal Trust

61.52%

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

82.39%

Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund

78.11%

As we can see, the Bexil Investment Trust was actually the best-performing fund of its peer group over the past five years when measured on a total return basis. This fund slightly beat the perennially strong performance of the Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund, and it substantially beat the performance of all of the other funds. This serves to support the thesis that I highlighted earlier in this article about dividend-paying stocks being fairly good things to hold during periods of inflation, given that this fund invests in such stocks and delivered the strongest performance of its peer group overall.

Recent Performance Updates

We previously discussed the Bexil Investment Trust on July 15, 2024. At the time, I noted that the Bexil Investment Trust struggled with performance over the trailing ten-year period, which is actually still the case today:

Here is the table showing the same data as above:

Fund Name

Trailing 10-Year Total Return

Bexil Investment Trust

135.87%

Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund

117.44%

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund

286.07%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

165.18%

Franklin Universal Trust

133.64%

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

152.44%

Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund

218.01%

In a contrary scenario to what occurred over the past five years, over the past ten years, the fund underperformed a number of its peers on a total return basis. The majority of this fund’s weakness comes from its generally poor performance during the years preceding the COVID-19 pandemic, as its strategy of focusing on dividend-paying stocks actually hurt it during this period. This is because dividend-paying stocks generally underperformed in general, a fact that AllianceBernstein even noted in the article that I linked earlier. From that article:

Dividend payers may not be top-of-mind for investors seeking high risk-adjusted returns, because the last decade hasn’t been kind to them. Over seven of the last 10 years through 2021, the MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index and the FTSE High Dividend Yield Index underperformed the S&P 500. High-dividend payers are often seen as old, stodgy companies, like utilities or food manufacturers, with limited growth potential. As investors flocked to high-flying technology and internet growth companies, dividend stocks seemed like antiquated investment options.

AllianceBernstein seems to believe that the next ten years will be kinder to dividend-paying companies than the decade from 2011 to 2021 was:

We think the last decade was an anomaly. When widening the lens, we found that stocks in the top two quintiles by dividend yield delivered superior returns over the past four decades. In our view, the second quintile is particularly attractive on a risk-adjusted basis, as it avoids riskier companies with unsustainably high payout ratios. And our research suggests that this long-term return potential comes with a bonus: attractive performance in an inflationary world.

I am inclined to agree to a point, particularly in the conclusion that dividend-paying stocks could be a good option for investors who are looking to generate an income from the assets that they have in their portfolios. One of the nice things about equities is that they represent ownership in an actual profit-making business, and they basically entitle owners to a portion of the company’s profits. A dividend is simply the company opting to actually share these profits with the equity holders instead of keeping them for itself. Typically, a company’s profits will increase with inflation as the company raises its prices in order to maintain its margins in such a world. In many cases, the company will then raise the amount of nominal currency that it distributes to its owners through the dividend. These dividend increases help to ensure that the investors can maintain their purchasing power, and in most cases, the common stock price will also increase in order for the company’s common stock to keep relatively stable multiples. Thus, the equities of these companies should do reasonably well in an inflationary environment, and I suspect that we will experience an inflationary environment in the United States going forward. Thus, the next decade should prove to be better for these stocks than the previous one was.

We actually saw this strategy play out for the Bexil Investment Trust, as the past five years were much better for the fund than were the five years preceding the pandemic. Thus, in this respect, the fund is much improved compared to its previous self, even though it did not really change its strategy very much.

As you may remember, back in July of 2024, the reported official rate of inflation was in decline, and expectations were that the Federal Reserve would soon start to aggressively reduce its benchmark interest rate. We have a very different situation today. This chart shows the reported rate of inflation in each of the past twelve months based on the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers:

We can see that the reported rate of inflation declined in both August and September 2024 before starting to reaccelerate. It is worth noting that September 2024 was when the Federal Reserve finally started to ease monetary policy and cut interest rates by a whopping 50 basis points. The Federal Reserve continued to cut at its remaining two meetings of the year, then stopped in December. As of right now, the benchmark federal funds target rate is 100 basis points lower than it was the last time that we discussed this fund.

It seems to be a somewhat mainstream view that whenever the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates, then yields fall all across the yield curve. However, this is not necessarily the case, and the events of the past several months prove it. As of right now, the yields of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note, the twenty-year U.S. Treasury bond, and the thirty-year U.S. Treasury bond are all higher than they were the last time that we discussed this fund:

U.S. Treasury Security

Yield on July 15, 2024

Yield on July 23, 2025

Ten-Year

4.23%

4.40%

Twenty-Year

4.56%

4.93%

Thirty-Year

4.46%

4.95%

This is mostly because the market is expecting that inflation will be somewhat higher going forward than it was expecting back in July 2024. As we saw earlier, U.S. Treasury bonds have a strongly negative correlation with inflation, so we would generally expect that yields would be higher today in spite of the Federal Reserve’s attempts to reduce interest rates.

As we just saw, inflation did start to decline during the first few months of 2025, but it has since bottomed out and started to rise. Both of the past two months showed an acceleration in the year-over-year rate of change in the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers. Thus, the market fears that inflation is not beaten yet, and rightly so, as the reported rate of inflation is now worse than it was in August 2024.

As we have been discussing, though, dividend-paying stocks, such as the ones that are included in the Bexil Investment Trust, exhibit a positive correlation with inflation. As such, we can assume that they would have delivered a positive performance since our last discussion, given that inflation appears to be showing signs of worsening.

This is indeed the case, although the Bexil Investment Trust did underperform the S&P 500 Index since our last discussion:

As we can see, the fund’s shares are up 4.79% since the date that my previous article on this fund was published. This is still a gain, but it is far worse than the 12.92% gain that the S&P 500 Index delivered over the same period. As just over twelve months have passed since our last discussion on the fund, a 4.79% gain is not a particularly great return. This would be a nice gain in a one-month period, but it is not particularly impressive over twelve months.

As we have already seen, though, it is usually best to look at total return as opposed to price return when evaluating the performance of closed-end funds. Here is the total return chart for the Bexil Investment Trust over the same period:

Here we can see that the Bexil Investment Trust actually managed to get pretty close to matching the performance of the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR) over the period in question. This is because the fund’s distributions were sufficient to make up the difference between the price performance of the two asset classes. The Bexil Investment Trust has a substantially higher yield than the S&P 500 Index, after all. Investors should take into account that this fund will deliver a higher proportion of its total returns in the form of distributions than would be the case for something such as an S&P 500 Index fund and design their portfolios accordingly. In particular, the fund’s distributions will be taxed at a higher rate than would be the case with the unrealized capital gains that an index fund would deliver. Of course, the taxation problem is easy enough to avoid by holding the fund’s shares in a retirement account as opposed to a taxable brokerage account.

About the Fund

The website for the Bexil Investment Trust states that the primary objective of the fund is to provide its investors with a high level of current income. Ordinarily, the provision of current income does not make much sense as an objective for an equity fund due to the fact that equities themselves are not income vehicles. As we saw earlier in this article, the S&P 500 Index only yields 1.13%. Even the MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index, which tracks the performance of some of the highest-yielding stocks that trade on American markets, only yields 3.04% at the current level. Neither one of these yields is especially impressive from an income perspective, which is due to the fact that equities mostly deliver their returns through capital appreciation. The yield is typically a secondary consideration in the minds of most investors who are purchasing equity securities.

The Bexil Investment Trust invests primarily in dividend-paying stocks, so we can assume that its portfolio as a whole will have a higher yield than that of the S&P 500 Index. After all, the S&P 500 Index does have high weightings for companies such as Amazon.com (AMZN) and Tesla (TSLA) that do not pay dividends. The S&P 500 Index also has very high allocations to companies such as NVIDIA (NVDA) that have yields well below that of the index as a whole. Thus, we can assume that the average dividend-paying stock will have a yield that is somewhat higher than that of the index. This chart shows the dividend yields of all of the fund’s current holdings:

Company Name

Current Dividend Yield

AutoZone (AZO)

n/a

NVR, Inc. (NVR)

n/a

United Rentals (URI)

0.89%

Snap-on Incorporated (SNA)

2.59%

Dillard’s (DDS)

5.26%

Williams-Sonoma (WSM)

1.44%

Lam Research Corporation (LRCX)

0.95%

Elevance Health (ELV)

2.31%

Essent Group (ESNT)

2.18%

NMI Holdings (NMIH)

n/a

UnitedHealth Group (UNH)

3.02%

UFP Industries (UFPI)

1.33%

Centerra Gold (CGAU)

2.71%

Kinross Gold (KGC)

0.74%

New Gold Inc. (NGD)

n/a

OceanaGold Corp. (OTCQX:OCANF)

0.82%

Rio Tinto plc (RIO)

6.22%

Wheaton Precious Metals Corp. (WPM)

0.70%

Credit Acceptance Corporation (CACC)

n/a

Matador Resources Company (MTDR)

2.48%

Valero Energy (VLO)

3.06%

Mueller Industries (MLI)

1.15%

Steel Dynamics (STLD)

1.53%

Crocs, Inc. (CROX)

n/a

Interactive Brokers Group, Inc. (IBKR)

0.51%

LPL Financial Holdings (LPLA)

0.32%

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL)

0.44%

Clear Secure, Inc. (YOU)

2.50%

Donnelley Financial Solutions (DFIN)

n/a

International Seaways, Inc. (INSW)

9.86%

Pool Corporation (POOL)

1.58%

We do see that a number of the fund’s holdings have yields that are much higher than those of the S&P 500 Index. However, there are a surprising number of companies in the fund’s portfolio that have lower-than-average yields. In particular, most of the gold miners and gold streaming companies have yields that are well below 1% and certainly below the average stock in the S&P 500 Index.

I have a very difficult time complaining about the presence of the gold miners in this fund’s portfolio, though, even though their yields are lower than that of the average stock in the market. This is mostly because of their exposure to gold prices, which have been rising dramatically lately. Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has substantially outperformed the S&P 500 Index year-to-date, as the shiny yellow metal is up a whopping 28.14% compared to the 8.12% price gain of the S&P 500 Index.

This impressive performance for gold has also had a positive impact on gold miners, which we can see by looking at the performance of the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index (GDX). This index is up 56.71% year-to-date, substantially outperforming both the S&P 500 Index and gold itself:

This is due to the fact that the profits of gold mining companies are strongly correlated with the price of gold, which makes a lot of sense.

As regular readers likely know, I am quite bullish on gold due to my view that inflation is likely to be a bigger problem going forward than what we have all become accustomed to over the past four decades since the 1980s. Gold has a 3.66 beta to the Consumer Price Index, so whenever inflation rises, gold rises at a faster rate. If gold prices are rising due to inflation in the economy amidst a U.S. dollar devaluation, then gold mining stocks should also rise over the long term. This is the biggest reason why I am not opposed to the Bexil Investment Trust having some exposure to gold mining companies, despite the fact that their yields are not really that impressive. It should be able to earn some capital gains from these stocks going forward. Furthermore, there is the fact that most of these gold mining companies had higher yields back at the start of the year, but their impressive gains since that time have reduced their yields because they have not raised their dividends nearly as rapidly as their share prices have gained.

Another thing that we notice by looking at the fund’s portfolio is that the Bexil Investment Trust has a very different portfolio from most other equity funds. For example, with the exception of Alphabet, there is very little technology exposure here. Indeed, the fund’s top sector is mining:

This is very different from the S&P 500 Index, as that index only has a 1.90% weighting to the materials sector. The bulk of the fund’s metals sector exposure comes from the gold miners that it holds, but Rio Tinto produces other products as well. For example, Rio Tinto produces iron ore, bauxite, aluminum, copper, molybdenum, and other metallic commodities. Thus, we do have exposure to commodities besides just gold here, and that could be a very good thing for diversification purposes. Commodities (including energy, industrial commodities, and gold) have a 1.77 beta with the Consumer Price Index, so they should also prove to be a reasonable holding should the United States end up devaluing the U.S. dollar to cover its unfunded liabilities. Thus, this fund actually appears to be very well positioned for a continued long-term weakening of the U.S. dollar (against real assets, not against other currencies, as many nations will likely weaken their fiat currencies going forward), which could make it a pretty good holding for anyone who is looking for an inflation hedge.

The fact that the Bexil Investment Trust has very limited exposure to the mega-capitalization technology companies that are currently trading with very high valuations due to expectations about the deployment of artificial intelligence could also be quite attractive, depending on what else an investor has in their portfolio. In particular, it could help to improve diversification across the portfolio, as most investors have significant exposure to the giant technology companies because most funds are highly weighted to them. The Bexil Investment Trust does not provide exposure to other assets that could perform differently, which may help an investor improve the overall risk profile and volatility of their portfolio.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Bexil Investment Trust is to provide its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a quarterly distribution of $0.2500 per share ($1.00 per year) to its investors. This gives the fund a 7.32% yield at the current share price.

The Bexil Investment Trust has not been particularly consistent with respect to its distributions, as it has changed them numerous times over the past ten years:

As we can see, the fund has paid distributions that have been as low as $0.1500 quarterly or as high as $0.5700 for a quarter (although this $0.5700 quarterly distribution was a one-time event as the fund sought to distribute a substantial amount of investment profit that it made in 2021). This distribution history might not be particularly attractive to someone who is looking for a stable source of income to use to pay their bills. The fact that this fund only pays a quarterly distribution as opposed to a monthly one also hurts it in the eyes of those investors. Based on the quarterly schedule and the fact that the distribution varies over time, this fund is better used as a supplemental source of income. We can think of it almost like a bonus from a job rather than as a regular salary.

Naturally, though, we want to see how well the fund is covering its distribution before we make an investment in it. For this purpose, we will have a look at the fund’s annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024. This is a much newer report than the one that was available to us at the time of our last discussion, so it is important to review it to get an update on how well the fund is doing financially.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the Bexil Investment Trust reported a net investment income of $95,276. That was nowhere close to enough to cover the $13,746,198 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period. Of course, we do not generally expect an equity fund to fully cover its distributions with net investment income. This is because the yield on equity tends to be so low that the dividends received by most equity funds are barely enough to cover the fund’s expenses.

The fund was, fortunately, able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the Bexil Investment Trust reported net realized gains of $24,764,837, which were partially offset by $1,251,400 of net unrealized losses:

We still see, though, that the fund’s net realized gains were more than sufficient to cover the net unrealized losses and the distributions that the fund paid out over the period. Thus, this fund easily covered its distributions with quite a lot of money left over. Investors clearly have nothing to worry about here. This fund is not having difficulties covering its distributions.

Valuation

Shares of the Bexil Investment Trust are currently trading at a massive 36.70% discount on net asset value. This is generally in line with the 36.41% discount that this fund’s shares have had on average over the past month, but this is a large enough discount that the fund is clearly very cheap compared to the value of the underlying assets.

The Bexil Investment Trust has historically traded at a very cheap valuation, with the shares having discounts of 27.88% to 40.38% on net asset value over the past three years. The average price over the trailing three-year period was a 35.07% discount on net asset value, which is a more expensive price than the fund has right now. As such, the Bexil Investment Trust does not appear to be especially expensive compared to its long-term history.

As might be expected, the Bexil Investment Trust is incredibly cheap compared to its peers, which we can see in this chart:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Bexil Investment Trust

(36.70%)

Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund

(9.09%)

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund

(3.45%)

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

(8.64%)

Franklin Universal Trust

(7.35%)

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

(6.40%)

Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund

(10.06%)

We can immediately see that all of the funds in the peer group are currently trading at prices that are lower than the actual value of their assets. The cheapest out of the peer group is the Bexil Investment Trust by a considerable margin, so it obviously appears that this fund is very reasonably priced compared to the peer average.

Overall, the current price of the Bexil Investment Trust is quite reasonable.

Expenses

The annual report for the Bexil Investment Trust states that the fund had an expense ratio of 1.61% for the full-year period that ended on December 31, 2024. The fund’s expense ratio may seem high compared to most exchange-traded index funds, but it is important to remember that this fund does employ leverage, and so it has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed. If we exclude those interest expenses, the fund’s expense ratio drops to 1.32%.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Bexil Investment Trust has a fairly unique strategy among closed-end funds, and this has positioned it quite well for the current and likely future market environments. The fund has a very different portfolio from that of many other funds on the market, as it has only very limited exposure to the mega-cap technology stocks and the generative artificial intelligence theme that is driving equities upward. This has not prevented it from performing well, though, as gold mining stocks have also done quite well this year, and the Bexil Investment Trust has a fairly substantial level of exposure to those companies. The fund should, overall, work quite well in an inflationary environment, and it seems likely that inflation will be a permanent companion in the economy over the long term. Thus, this fund may be a good way for investors to protect themselves while achieving diversification and a high level of income.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AEye: No Better Place To Be Than In Nvidia - Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I'm bullish on AEye, Inc. (NASDAQ:LIDR), a tech company that manufactures lidar sensors, following Thursday, aka today's, massive 240% surge at market open, which has now cooled down a bit to 149%, as shown below. The stock got itself a piece of game-changing news: the company's Apollo LiDAR system is now integrated into the NVIDIA (NVDA) DRIVE AGX platform, part of Nvidia's DRIVE ecosystem.

A partnership with Nvidia puts AEye on the map in the world of tech and autonomous driving, which is experiencing a gold rush-like race this year as robotaxis become more commercially viable, from Google's Waymo to Tesla's robotaxi. Management issued a press release this morning announcing the partnership and also quoting CEO Matt Fisch, saying:

We are thrilled to now be officially certified as a part of NVIDIA's DRIVE AGX platform, a strong validation of Apollo's best-in-class capabilities. Apollo's industry-leading 1-kilometer range and compact form factor make it a standout solution across every market we serve. Now that Apollo is a part of the NVIDIA DRIVE ecosystem, we have a powerful channel to scale commercially across the automotive industry.

The partnership's impact is already priced into the stock after this morning's surge, but I don't think it's a one-trick pony. This feels more like a Navitas (NVTS) situation; recall the time back in May when Navitas closed a partnership with Nvidia, which caused the stock price to surge 240%. Back then, everyone and their grandmother were holding their breath for the crash that never really came; the stock continued to be volatile but showed an overall upward trajectory because getting picked up by Nvidia boosts visibility in a big way. Since I said buy Navitas on its way up, the stock is up 70%, as shown below.

AEye is now heading into its next phase of growth, particularly when considering how small-scaled the business was up until now. I'm expecting more deals to come AEye's way in the LiDAR market, which is estimated to grow at a CAGR of 20.91% from 2025 to 2034.

AEye now has a software-defined sensor that fits perfectly into the future of connected, upgradeable vehicles. The company also emphasizes its AEye's 4Sight Intelligent Sensing Platform, which "enables... patented bistatic architecture to keep transmit and receive channels separate, allowing optimization for both." Even with the rally, I think AEye's share price looks cheap when you zoom out and consider the long-term potential. The company's market cap remains modest, especially when compared to other LiDAR peers with similar technology but greater scale. Now that Apollo is officially part of the NVIDIA DRIVE ecosystem, AEye has a real shot at landing direct design wins with major automakers, which could be a transformational revenue event. It's also worth mentioning that AEye was getting more traction even before this deal, announcing in June that a major transportation OEM had selected them for a potential $30M opportunity anticipated sometime over the next 24-36 months. If more OEMs integrate Apollo into a production vehicle, it could dramatically re-rate the stock. I initiate my coverage with a buy.

What Is AEye?

AEye is a small LiDAR company that most investors wouldn't have heard of before today. They build high-performance sensors for things like self-driving cars, smart traffic systems, and even industrial automation. Their main product, Apollo, helps vehicles "see" far ahead by bouncing laser pulses off objects and building a super-detailed 3D map in real time. However, what makes AEye stand out is that its technology is software-defined, allowing it to be essentially updated like an app rather than requiring hardware upgrades. They make money by selling these sensors to automakers, mobility companies, and governments rolling out smart infrastructure, and they're now trying to scale that up big time, especially with the new Nvidia deal.

Of course, AEye is still in the early stages of commercializing its technology, but recent financials do show some signs of progress ahead of this deal. In Q1 2025, the company reported revenue of $64,000, up from $20,000 a year ago quarter, which translates to a 200% increase. GAAP net loss was $8 million, down from $35.5 million the year prior. They underwent some aggressive cost-cutting that resulted in operating expenses dropping by nearly 75% to $6.8 million.

Cash burn for the quarter came in around $8 million, with full-year burn expected to land closer to $20 to $22 million after adjusting for one-time items. As of March, AEye had $25.9 million in cash on hand and access to additional credit, giving it enough liquidity for a cash runway to operate well into mid-2026. The company's balance sheet is pretty healthy, in my opinion, considering that the company's cash outweighs its debt.

Details Of The Deal With Nvidia

In the press release, management announced that its flagship Apollo LiDAR system is now fully integrated into the NVIDIA DRIVE AGX platform. It serves as a critical backbone for autonomous vehicle development. But what does this mean exactly? This means that its sensor is now compatible, but more importantly, certified to work seamlessly within Nvidia's autonomous driving ecosystem. DRIVE AGX is a leading AI computing platform used by some of the top automakers to power self-driving and advanced driver-assistance systems; it includes features such as lane assistance, automatic braking, and lane switching.

This is both a technical milestone and a commercial launchpad for new deals in the future. We need to remember that Nvidia has a global network of top-tier automakers, many of whom are actively building the next generation of self-driving and ADAS-equipped vehicles. So it's a major vote of confidence from Nvidia, and it gives AEye a legitimate path toward any meaningful market penetration.

Let's look at the tech behind Apollo, too, starting with sensors. Apollo boasts a 1-kilometer detection range, a very compact and power-efficient form factor, and most importantly, a software-defined architecture. This architecture is something we need to address; as AEye continues to enhance its performance and release updates, they won't need to swap hardware. Any updates going forward can be done remotely. The design is very forward-compatible and something the auto industry is heading towards. Think of Tesla (TSLA) vehicles and how many updates and subscriptions their customers receive. We can take the following as an example: AEye can integrate its Apollo system behind the windshield of vehicles. Traditionally, this area is off limits, but due to the compactness of the LiDAR, OEM manufacturers are becoming more flexible in this design aspect.

Yes, the stock surged triple-digits on the news, and that makes it a riskier investment to jump into at current levels. Nonetheless, there are healthy fundamentals at play here, too. I feel AEye can convert this momentum into concrete design wins with automakers, and having Nvidia's backing could be more reason for these automakers to turn their way. On top of that, management actually teased a new product line called OPTIS. This is aimed at applying AEye's AI-driven perception tools beyond just automotive uses. They want to look more into smart infrastructure and logistics, as well as safety applications.

In short, the integration with Nvidia isn't just a press release for investors to get momentarily excited about. It validates AEye's technology, meaning their tech is truly worth something moving forward. The only thing we need to watch for moving forward is execution, especially since AEye is a micro-cap player.

Valuation & What's Next?

AEye doesn't yet have meaningful earnings, so traditional valuation metrics like P/E or EV/EBITDA aren't the most helpful. However, based on forward-looking revenue, we can gauge some relative valuation. The company trades at a forward EV to Sales of 1.22, which appears to be well below the sector median of 3.24 on Seeking Alpha. On the other hand, its Price/Sales (TTM) ratio stands at a lofty 46.06, compared to a sector median of just 3.39. It shows how early stage the company is, with minimal revenue but extremely high expectations. The market capitalization is now $73.5 million. Shares are trading at $2.81 today.

Keep in mind that this is a relatively young company, having been founded in 2013 in the San Francisco Bay Area. They consistently aimed to develop smarter, software-defined LiDAR systems for autonomous vehicles, and the market is now ready to adopt such solutions. AEye initially went public through SPAC back in 2021; since then, the stock is down a little over 99%.

The company is scheduled to report earnings for Q2 at the end of the month, July 31st to be specific, so I'm excited to see what management has to say about the partnership and future plans going forward. Given the company's relative youth, I view this price as an early entry point.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
